coaching.subject: accruing_since timestamp origin and assignment

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on compute callable runtime exception handling: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: compute callable runtime exception handling

coaching.subject: acceptance criteria gaps and uncovered deliverables

coaching.subject: lens field type, vocabulary, and semantics

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `compute` callable's signature and contract: what arguments it receives, what it returns when data is available (`MetricValue`), and what it returns or raises when data is absent (triggering `Unavailable` construction with `reason` + `accruing_since`).
evidence: Plan body: 'MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' — compute listed with no signature
Approach: 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since) rather than being absent' — states the outcome but not the mechanism
ABSENCE: no protocol specifying compute's return type, input parameters, or the decision rule that converts its output into MetricValue vs. Unavailable
findings.finding: The `compute` field is named in `MetricSpec` but its callable signature — what arguments it receives, what it must return when data is present, and whether it returns `Unavailable`/`None`/raises when data is absent — is never specified. The plan says 'a metric with no data returns `Unavailable`' without defining whether `compute` itself produces `Unavailable`, returns `None` (and the registry wraps it), or raises.
findings.location: Files section / registry.py — `MetricSpec (…compute)`; Approach — 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
scenarios: One implementer has compute() return None and the registry wraps it; another has compute() return Unavailable directly. Both satisfy the AC but produce incompatible registry iteration behavior.
The AC-2 test asserts Unavailable with reason+accruing_since but cannot verify the wrapping rule without a spec, so the test may pass for wrong reasons.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'compute: Callable[[], MetricValue | None] — returns a MetricValue when data is present, or None when unavailable; the registry wraps a None return into Unavailable(reason=<MetricSpec.unavailable_reason>, accruing_since=<MetricSpec.accruing_since>). Exceptions propagate uncaught (or are caught and re-raised as a distinct ErrorValue — TBD).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the source of `accruing_since` in `Unavailable`: specify whether it is a static field on `MetricSpec` (the date the metric was introduced, set at registry definition time) or dynamically computed, and update the AC or Approach section accordingly.
evidence: Approach section: 'Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)' — field listed but origin not stated
AC2: 'unit test asserts both fields present' — verifiable only if both are defined; accruing_since origin is missing
MetricSpec fields listed as '(id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' — no accruing_since field on the spec itself
ABSENCE: no specification of whether accruing_since is a MetricSpec-level config (static date) or a dynamic value produced at render time
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `Unavailable` carries an ISO `accruing_since` field (and AC2 asserts it is non-empty), but never defines where this timestamp originates — whether it is a static field on `MetricSpec` (the metric's introduction date, set at definition time), dynamically computed (e.g., 'now' when compute returns no data), or read from some external record. This leaves the field semantically undefined and the AC unverifiable.
findings.location: Approach section; AC2 — 'A metric with no data returns Unavailable carrying a non-empty reason and an ISO accruing_since'
scenarios: Implementer sets accruing_since=datetime.now(UTC).isoformat() — the field becomes meaningless (it changes every call, not a 'since when has this been accruing' marker)
Implementer omits accruing_since from MetricSpec and cannot construct Unavailable for the AC without inventing a convention
Downstream consumers parse accruing_since to compute 'how long have we been waiting for this signal' — a dynamic 'now' breaks that use entirely
findings.suggested_fix: Add `accruing_since: str` to `MetricSpec` as a required ISO-date field (e.g. '2025-01-15') set at registry definition time, representing when the metric began accruing. `Unavailable` then reads it from the spec. This makes the field durable, meaningful, and trivially testable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `MetricValue`'s fields in the plan (at minimum: the computed value, plus whether it carries `source` and `confidence` labels redundantly with `MetricSpec` or relies on the spec for those).
evidence: Plan body: 'MetricValue/Unavailable result types' — both named as deliverables
Approach and AC-2 define Unavailable fields (reason, accruing_since) explicitly
ABSENCE: no field specification for MetricValue anywhere in the plan (no value field, no source/confidence propagation spec, no numeric type)
findings.finding: `MetricValue` is named as a result type in the Files/registry.py description alongside `Unavailable`, but its fields are never defined in the plan. Unlike `Unavailable` (whose fields `reason` and `accruing_since` are named in the Approach and AC-2), `MetricValue` has no specified shape.
scenarios: MetricValue is implemented without carrying source+confidence labels, contradicting the plan's stated purpose of keeping those labels on every value for downstream aggregation.
AC-3's authoritative rollup test operates on MetricValue instances but cannot assert their shape without a spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback specification for `compute` raising an exception: either exceptions propagate (and the caller must handle), or the registry catches them and returns `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=...)` — whichever is intended must be stated and tested.
evidence: Approach specifies Unavailable for 'no data' case only
ABSENCE: no specification of what the registry does when compute() raises (propagate, swallow, return a distinct ErrorValue, log-and-return-Unavailable)
Tests section only tests the no-data path, not the error path
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a `compute` callable that raises an exception at runtime (as distinct from returning no data). The plan only defines the no-data → `Unavailable` path; a runtime error in `compute` has no specified handling.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
scenarios: A compute function that calls an unavailable sidecar raises a network error; with no specified fallback the registry either crashes the CLI or silently drops the metric — both contradict the graceful-unavailability contract.
The 'command is written once and never modified as new signals accrue' guarantee breaks if new signals can crash the command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-containment contract for `compute` callables: define whether an exception degrades to `Unavailable(reason='error: …', accruing_since=…)` or propagates, and add an AC or test case asserting that behavior.
evidence: Plan states 'compute' is a field of MetricSpec but gives no error-handling contract for it
Approach section: 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable' — but covers only the no-data case, not the error/exception case
The codebase pattern for best-effort reads (e.g. sidecar.py: 'except Exception: logger.warning(…); return None') is well-established but not prescribed for this compute callable
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when a MetricSpec's `compute` callable raises an exception (e.g., git unavailable, sidecar unreadable, snapshot missing). The render loop's error-containment contract is absent, leaving a real failure path undefined — compute errors could either propagate and break the entire render pass, or silently produce undefined behavior.
scenarios: git is unavailable at render time — a structural metric's compute raises subprocess.CalledProcessError; the render loop behavior is undefined
A sidecar read fails transiently; the compute callable throws; the whole metrics command fails instead of returning Unavailable with reason='error'
A new metric's compute callable has a bug; the bug silently breaks unrelated metrics if exceptions propagate through the shared render loop
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'A compute callable that raises is caught by the render loop and treated as Unavailable(reason=f"compute error: {exc}", accruing_since=…); this keeps a single-metric failure isolated.' Add a unit test case asserting this.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `lens` field's type and vocabulary (or acceptable values/examples) in the plan — either an enum, a string taxonomy, or a typed callable — so AC-1's assertion is verifiable against a known contract.
evidence: Plan body: 'MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' — lens is listed but never further defined
AC-1: 'every REGISTRY entry has a unique id, a lens, a source, and a confidence' — asserted but vocabulary of lens is absent
ABSENCE: no section in the plan defines what a valid lens value looks like (enum, string, callable, object)
findings.finding: The `lens` field is required in every REGISTRY entry (AC-1 asserts its presence) and listed as a `MetricSpec` field, but neither its type, vocabulary, nor semantics are defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer has no specification to conform to.
findings.location: Files section / registry.py — `MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)`; AC-1 — `every REGISTRY entry has a … lens`
scenarios: Two implementers produce incompatible lens representations (e.g. one uses a string key, another uses a callable), breaking the registry iteration the command depends on.
The unit test AC-1 asserts lens presence but cannot assert correctness without a spec, leaving the field permanently under-validated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Approach section, e.g.: '`lens` is a string from a fixed set (e.g. `{"velocity", "quality", "coverage", "health"}`) or a callable that groups metrics for rendering; the AC-1 test asserts `lens is not None`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC2) that verifies a `MetricValue` result from a metric whose compute returns data carries the expected `source` and `confidence` labels, covering the success-path result type.
evidence: Plan body: 'MetricValue/Unavailable result types' listed as a deliverable in registry.py
AC1 checks REGISTRY entry fields; AC2 checks Unavailable fields; AC3 checks is_authoritative — none reference MetricValue
findings.finding: `MetricValue` is named in the plan body as a deliverable result type alongside `Unavailable`, but no acceptance criterion verifies its existence, shape, or behavior — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Files / registry.py description; Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
scenarios: An implementer could omit MetricValue entirely (returning only Unavailable or a raw dict) and all three ACs would still pass, leaving the stated API contract unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'A metric whose compute returns data yields a `MetricValue` carrying a non-None `source` and `confidence`; a metric with no data yields `Unavailable` carrying a non-empty `reason` and an ISO `accruing_since`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1 to also assert that every REGISTRY entry has a callable `compute` field (e.g., `callable(spec.compute)` for all specs), aligning the criterion with the full `MetricSpec` definition.
evidence: AC1 text: 'every REGISTRY entry has a unique id, a lens, a source, and a confidence'
Plan body (registry.py): 'MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' — compute is the mechanism that drives Unavailable vs MetricValue
findings.finding: AC1 checks that every REGISTRY entry has `id`, `lens`, `source`, and `confidence` — but the plan defines `MetricSpec` as `(id, lens, source, confidence, compute)`, omitting `compute` from the verified fields, leaving the core functional field uncovered.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1; Files / registry.py ('MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)')
scenarios: A REGISTRY entry with a None or missing `compute` field would pass AC1 yet make AC2's Unavailable test meaningless, since the mechanism producing Unavailable is never validated at the registry level.
findings.suggested_fix: AC1 revised: '…every REGISTRY entry has a unique id, a lens, a source, a confidence, and a callable compute — asserted by iterating the registry and checking all five fields plus `len(ids) == len(set(ids))`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `source` and `confidence` as explicit `Literal` types or `Enum` members (not inline set literals), and derive `is_authoritative` from the enum membership so adding a new source type requires exactly one edit in one place.
evidence: 'source' in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and 'confidence' in {high, classified} ride on every value
The plan's goal: 'a new signal lights up by populating its source with no CLI change' — but adding a new source *type* still requires editing is_authoritative and the enum set
findings.finding: The `source` taxonomy (`{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`) and `is_authoritative` business rule are hardcoded in `registry.py` with no mechanism to extend them without modifying the core file, despite the plan's stated goal that new signals accrue without CLI changes.
scenarios: A new signal category (e.g., 'ml_inferred') is added and the developer must hunt down all hardcoded source-set references in registry.py to update is_authoritative logic
A future contributor adds a source value not in the known set, silently bypassing the authoritative filter with no validation error
findings.suggested_fix: Use a Python `Enum` (e.g., `class MetricSource(str, Enum): STRUCTURAL='structural'; GIT='git'; ...`) with an `AUTHORITATIVE` frozenset defined against enum members; `is_authoritative` checks membership in that frozenset. New sources are added to the enum and explicitly opted in/out of the frozenset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or `AGENTS.md` section documenting the metric registry pattern: what MetricSpec fields mean, the source/confidence taxonomy, the Unavailable contract, and the protocol for adding a new metric or source type.
evidence: No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update is listed in the Files section or Approach
The Approach section contains the only written rationale for why this pattern exists and how it should be extended — this rationale lives only in the ephemeral plan text
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel foundational architectural contract (declarative metric registry, Unavailable sentinel, source/confidence label taxonomy, is_authoritative segregation) with no mention of capturing the design decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc.
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new metric and doesn't know whether to add a new source type or reuse an existing one, because the taxonomy rationale isn't documented
The is_authoritative contract is subtly changed by a contributor who doesn't realize it's a load-bearing invariant for aggregation correctness

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding unit test) asserting that when a compute function returns data, the resulting `MetricValue` carries the `source` and `confidence` fields inherited from the `MetricSpec` — verifying the labeled-value contract on the happy path.
evidence: Plan says 'source and confidence labels ride on every value so downstream aggregation can keep high-confidence structural series apart from fuzzy backfills' — but no AC or test description exercises a successful compute path producing a MetricValue with populated source+confidence fields.
AC1 checks registry structure, AC2 checks Unavailable, AC3 checks is_authoritative — the MetricValue success path is absent from all three.
findings.finding: The plan's tests and ACs cover the Unavailable (no-data) path and the is_authoritative helper, but there is no described test or AC verifying the happy path: that when a compute function returns data, the resulting MetricValue carries the source and confidence labels that form the core downstream-aggregation contract. The phrase 'source+confidence labels ride on every value' is central to the design but is never asserted in a test.
findings.location: Tests section and AC list (no AC for MetricValue happy path)
scenarios: A compute function returns valid data but the implementer forgets to copy source/confidence from the MetricSpec onto the MetricValue; all existing tests pass but the aggregation contract is silently broken at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A metric whose compute returns a valid payload produces a `MetricValue` with `source` and `confidence` matching the registry entry — unit test constructs a minimal MetricSpec with a compute stub that returns data and asserts `result.source == spec.source` and `result.confidence == spec.confidence`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm at least two additional concrete call-sites (beyond the single render loop) exist or are named within this plan's done-definition that justify the declarative registry abstraction; otherwise scope the initial implementation to a simpler structure (e.g. a plain list of named tuples in the rendering module) promotable to a registry when a second consumer arrives.
evidence: grep for MetricSpec, REGISTRY (metrics package), MetricValue, Unavailable, is_authoritative: zero existing call-sites in src/rebar/
Plan names exactly one consumer: 'rendering iterates the registry' — no second or third consumer identified in this plan's scope
Rule-of-Three requires >=3 existing call-sites or a current done-definition with multiple distinct consumers before introducing a registry abstraction
findings.finding: The plan proposes a declarative REGISTRY abstraction (MetricSpec, REGISTRY dict, MetricValue/Unavailable types) but names only one consumer ('rendering iterates the registry'), failing the Rule-of-Three bar for introducing a registry abstraction. Without two additional concrete call-sites within the current plan's done-definition, the registry layer is premature — the same behavior could be implemented as a simple list/function in the rendering command and promoted to a registry when a second or third consumer materialises.
findings.location: Approach section: 'A declarative registry (not per-metric scattered code) is what makes the command complete-once: rendering iterates the registry'
scenarios: The registry ships with one consumer; the second and third signals never materialise, leaving a three-file abstraction maintained for a single render loop.
The rendering command is the only caller; future contributors bypass the registry and scatter metric logic anyway because the pattern has no enforced consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or defer the `is_authoritative` / authoritative-rollup helper to the ticket that introduces the first aggregation consumer; for now the `source` and `confidence` labels on `MetricValue` are sufficient for a consumer to filter — no helper is needed until there is a caller.
evidence: grep for 'is_authoritative', 'rollup', 'backfill_classified': zero matches in src/rebar/
The plan's own scope is the registry + unavailable-state + labels contract; no aggregation pipeline or dashboard consumer is named in the plan's Files, Approach, or AC sections

AC3 exercises a helper that does not yet have a caller; the plan's stated benefit ('so downstream aggregation can keep high-confidence structural series apart') is a future-tense rationale, not a current done-definition
findings.finding: The `is_authoritative(source)` helper and the 'authoritative aggregation rollup' (AC3: 'a mixed input yields a series containing only source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}') are tested in isolation but serve no named current command or pipeline within this plan's scope. No existing or in-scope caller is identified that performs such aggregation. This is a YAGNI violation — speculative infrastructure written for a consumer that has not yet been defined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 3: '`is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and an authoritative aggregation excludes classified values — unit test asserts a mixed input yields a series containing only `source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}`'
scenarios: The rollup helper ships with zero callers; it accrues maintenance cost and test overhead for a use-case not yet defined.
A future aggregation pipeline is built differently (e.g. a SQL query, a pandas filter) making the helper dead code.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `is_authoritative` and the rollup helper from `registry.py` and its AC. The `source` field on `MetricValue` is self-describing; a caller can write `source in {'structural','git','sidecar','snapshot'}` inline when it needs to. Reinstate the helper (and its AC) in the ticket that first needs aggregation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/metrics/__init__.py` to the Files section (or confirm the repo's pytest config uses `importmode=importlib` and no `__init__.py` is needed for subdirectory discovery).
evidence: tests/unit/ directory listing shows no metrics/ subdirectory exists
No tests/unit/metrics/__init__.py is named in the plan's Files section

All other test subdirectories under tests/unit/ (grounding/, io/, rebar_reconciler/, workflow/) are directory entries — their __init__.py status is not verified but the pattern is consistent with needing package initialization for discovery
findings.finding: The Files section lists `tests/unit/metrics/test_registry.py` as a new file but does not list `tests/unit/metrics/__init__.py`. The `tests/unit/` directory currently has no `metrics/` subdirectory. If the project's pytest configuration uses `importmode=prepend` or otherwise requires `__init__.py` files for test-subdirectory discovery (as many projects do), the test file will not be collected and the ACs will silently not run.
scenarios: pytest runs, the metrics/ subdirectory is not a Python package, test_registry.py is not collected, all ACs pass vacuously with zero tests run
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/metrics/__init__.py` to the Files list as an empty new file, matching the pattern used by other test subdirectories in this repo.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
